revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:جون برين (مؤرخ)]][[arz:جون برين (مؤرخ من المملكه المتحده)]][[en:John Breen (scholar)]][[fr:John Breen]][[ja:ジョン・ブリーン]] {{Infobox person|image=File:John-Breen-(scholar).jpg|birth_date=1956; {{birth date|1956|3|3}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|United Kingdom|qid=Q145|1=United Kingdom}}|occupation={{ill|editing staff|qid=Q876864|1=editing staff}}; {{ill|historian|qid=Q201788|1=Historian}}; {{ill|japanologist|qid=Q15991218|1=japanologist}}; {{ill|university teacher|qid=Q1622272|1=university teacher}}|given_name={{ill|Q4925477|qid=Q4925477|1=John (given name)}}}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=April 2022}} [[File:John-Breen-(scholar).jpg|thumb|250 px|John Breen at a conference in Tokyo, February 2011]] '''John Lawrence Breen''' (born 3 March 1956)<ref>Library of Congress authority file, [http://errol.oclc.org/laf/n2002-55791.html John Breen, n2002-55791]</ref> is a British academic and [[:en:Japanologist|Japanologist]]. He is a specialist in Japanese history at the {{nihongo|{{ill|International Research Center for Japanese Studies|en|International Research Center for Japanese Studies|qid=Q6052724|lt=International Research Center for Japanese Studies|1=International Research Center for Japanese Studies}}|国際日本文化研究センター|Nichibunken}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. He writes in English and Japanese on the {{ill|History of Shinto|en|History of Shinto|qid=Q107199269|lt=History of Shinto|1=History of Shinto}} and the imperial institution.
==Early life== Breen was awarded his BA at the {{ill|University of Cambridge|en|University of Cambridge|qid=Q35794|lt=University of Cambridge|1=University of Cambridge}} in 1979. He earned a Ph.D. in 1993 at Cambridge.
==Career == From 1985 through 2008, Breen was a Lecturer, Senior Lecturer and Reader in Japanese at the [[:en:School of Oriental and African Studies|School of Oriental and African Studies]] in London. He is currently Professor at the International Research Centre for Japanese Studies.<ref>International Research Centre for Japanese Studies, [http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/research/faculty/staff1/breen02_e.html faculty CV] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120307185846/http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/research/faculty/staff1/breen02_e.html |date=2012-03-07 }}</ref> He is also Editor-in-Chief of the [[:en:Peer-reviewed journal|Peer-reviewed journal]] ''Japan Review''.<ref>H-Net: [http://h-net.msu.edu/cgi-bin/logbrowse.pl?trx=vx&list=H-Japan&month=1010&week=b&msg=QZmLKcvORV0x2hEV8Cj05w&user=&pw= Japan Review, Vol. 22] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304053810/http://h-net.msu.edu/cgi-bin/logbrowse.pl?trx=vx&list=H-Japan&month=1010&week=b&msg=QZmLKcvORV0x2hEV8Cj05w&user=&pw= |date=2016-03-04 }}</ref>
Breen's critical examination of religious practices in Japan has been informed by his historical research. {{ill|Historicity|en|Historicity|qid=Q1517710|lt=Historicity|1=Historicity}} is construed as a fundamental component of Breen's view of Shinto.<ref name="rambelli">Rambelli, Fabio. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fb20010715a2.html "Dismantling stereotypes surrounding Japan's sacred entities,"] ''Japan Times.'' July 15, 2001</ref>
Breen's work on Shinto is influenced by the writings of {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|en|Toshio Kuroda|qid=Q130309955|lt=Toshio Kuroda|1=Toshio Kuroda}}.<ref name="rambelli"/> As most contemporary historians, he holds a more moderate position. While Kuroda denied Shinto was more than a japanized version of Buddhism, Breen and Teeuwen argue there was a pre-modern, indigenous tradition of worship, mythology and shrines, even if indeed Shinto as an organized religion was yet to be born.<ref name=lande>{{cite news|last=Lande|first=Aasuv|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/publications/jjrs/pdf/856.pdf|publisher=Nanzan Institute for Religion and Culture|accessdate=4 February 2011|archive-date=19 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110719235933/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/publications/jjrs/pdf/856.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref>
Breen has also written articles in English and Japanese and a book in Japanese on the transformation of the imperial institution in the 19th century.
==Selected works== In a statistical overview derived from writings by and about John Breen, {{ill|OCLC|en|OCLC|qid=Q190593|lt=OCLC|1=OCLC}}/{{ill|WorldCat|en|WorldCat|qid=Q846596|lt=WorldCat|1=WorldCat}} encompasses roughly 10+ works in 30 publications in 1 language and 1,000+ library holding.<ref>[http://www.oclc.org/research/activities/identities/default.htm WorldCat Identities] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101230150412/http://www.oclc.org/research/activities/identities/default.htm |date=2010-12-30 }}: [http://www.worldcat.org/identities/lccn-n2002-55791 Breen, John 1956- ]</ref> {{dynamic list}} Books
* ''Japanese simplified'' (1987) * ''Japan and Christianity: Impacts and Responses'' (1996) (edited with Mark Williams) * ''Japanese in Three Months'' (1997) * ''Shinto in History: Ways of the kami'' (2000) (edited with Mark Teeuwen) * Inoue Nobutaka et al., Shintō: a short history (2002) (co-translated and adapted with Mark Teeuwen) * ''Yasukuni, the War Dead and the Struggle for Japan's Past'' (2008) (edited) * ''A 11 History of Shinto'' (2010) (co-authored with Mark Teeuwen) * "Girei to kenryoku: tennno no Meiji ishin" (2010)
;Book chapters (recent chapters in English only) *“Popes, bishops, and war criminals: reflections on Catholics and Yasukuni in post-war Japan,” in Michael Bathgate ed., Course Reader 10: “Religion in modern Asia: Tradition, state and society,” The Asia Pacific Journal; Japan Focus *“The nation’s shrine: conflict and commemoration at Yasukuni, modern Japan’s shrine to the war dead,” in Tsang, Rachel and Eric Taylor Woods eds. The cultural politics of nationalism and nation-building: ritual and performance in the forging of nations" (2014) *“Yasukuni shrine: ritual and memory,” in Sven Saaler & Justin Aukema eds., Course Reader 7: “The Politics of Memory in Japan and East Asia,” The Asia Pacific Journal; Japan Focus (2013) *“’Fine words indeed’: Yasukuni and the narrative fetishism of war,” Inken Prohl and John Nelson eds., Handbook of contemporary Japanese religions (2012) *“Shinto,” in Helmut Anheier, Mark Jurgensmeyer ed., Encyclopedia of Global Studies (2012) *“Voices of rage: six paths to the problem of Yasukuni,” in Roy Starrs ed., Politics and religion in modern Japan: red sun, white lotus (2012) *“Mourning and violence in the Land of Peace: Reflections on Yasukuni,” in Breen and Yamada eds. Understanding contemporary Japan (2011)
;Articles (recent articles in English only) *“Resurrecting the sacred land of Japan: the state of Shinto in the 21st century,” Japanese Journal of Religious Studies, 37, 2 (2010) *“’Shinto is the great way of the universe’: historical notes on Shinto-Christian negotiations,” Japan Mission Journal, 63, 4 (2009) *“’The danger is ever present’: Catholic critiques of Yasukuni shrine in post-war Japan,’ Japan Mission Journal, 63, 2 (2009)
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== * {{ill|Fabio Rambelli|en|Fabio Rambelli|qid=Q5427860|lt=Rambelli, Fabio|1=Fabio Rambelli}}. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fb20010715a2.html "Dismantling stereotypes surrounding Japan's sacred entities,"] ''Japan Times.'' July 15, 2001; book review excerpted from ''Monumenta Nipponica,'' 56:2.
